返回本批次

伊朗的情报失败

The Intelligence Failure in Iran

www.theatlantic.com 作者 JumpCrisscross 分数 61 评论 85 2026/04/06 19:21
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 2005年，一个由立法者和安全专家组成的两党委员会得出结论：“情报界在战前对伊拉克大规模杀伤性武器的几乎所有判断都是完全错误的。”美国间谍告诉乔治·W·布什总统，萨达姆·侯赛因已经重新制定了核武器计划，伊拉克拥有生物武器和移动生产设施，以及化学武器库存。这些假定的事实成为美国入侵和八年占领的基础。 “战争结束后，这一点都无法得到证实，”该委员会发现。 “这是一次重大的情报失败。”如果一个类似的专家小组仔细审查当前伊朗战争的准备情况，他们的评估可能会是这样的：情报界对伊朗攻击美国及其盟友的能力和意图的战前判断是准确和一致的。与特朗普总统为证明其决定合理性而所说的相反，情报显示伊朗政权并不准备使用核武器；它没有能够到达美国的弹道导弹；为了应对美国的军事攻击，伊朗可能会袭击波斯湾邻国，并试图关闭霍尔木兹海峡，从而引发全球经济危机。所有这些在战前就已为人所知，并提交给了特朗普总统。这是情报方面的一次成功。特朗普的“远征”，他称之为他第二任期内最大规模的美国军事行动，引发了一系列可怕的事件。伊朗现在控制了该海峡，计划向该海峡的船只收取通行费，并可以管理对制造业至关重要的石油、天然气、化肥和化学品的全球流动。特朗普声称要取代的政权仍然掌握在强硬派手中，他们对伊朗人民的镇压将因在世界唯一超级大国的斩首袭击中幸存下来而得到加强。海湾邻国的生计依赖​​于出口能源和为人们创造安全的旅游、生活和工作场所，它们将积累新武器，并重新考虑与美国的战略伙伴关系。二十年前，一位总统接受了事实证明是错误的信息，灾难随之而来。今天，总统无视被证明是正确的评估，可预见的事情就发生了。那里也存在情报失误，只是不是我们习惯看到的那种。1961 年，约翰·F·肯尼迪总统在弗吉尼亚州兰利的中央情报局总部向中央情报局工作人员发表讲话时说道，“你的成功无人知晓，你的失败却被大肆宣扬。”从那以后，每当情报官员因重大失误而被指责时，他们都会悲伤地援引这一不言而喻的道理。情报失败的故事情节为人们所熟知，其中包括忽视“连接各个点”的分析师、被夸大或撒谎的消息来源引诱的案件官员、以及扭曲模糊信息以符合他们想要的结果的政客。这就是伊拉克战争前几个月发生的事情。史诗愤怒行动的前奏彻底颠覆了这种说法。间谍们认为这是正确的，但总统却走了另一个方向。情报界在伊拉克大规模杀伤性武器问题上的失败引发了系统性变革，旨在防止类似的糟糕电话再次发生。在许多方面，这些改革都取得了成效。但他们无法解释决策者受到先前军事成功的诱惑，认为美国武装部队在他的灵感和也许是神圣的指挥下永远不会失败。特朗普的一些盟友批评他没有像布什政府那样公开宣扬战争。但如果他准确地提供了情报，事实就会反对攻击伊朗——或者至少在外交选择用尽之前不发动攻击。也许这就是为什么总统忽视并后来歪曲了他的顾问告诉他的话。 阅读：特朗普的命运选择“该政权已经拥有能够打击欧洲和我们的本地和海外基地的导弹，并且很快就会拥有能够到达我们美丽的美国的导弹，”特朗普在 3 月 2 日在白宫举行的荣誉勋章仪式上说道。但国防情报局得出的结论是，伊朗要到 2035 年才能制造出能够击中美国的导弹，而且只有到那时它才能实现决心这样做，但分析师得出的结论是事实并非如此。几周后，国家情报总监图尔西·加巴德（Tulsi Gabbard）——绝非非政治性总统顾问的典范——在国会作证时报告说，伊朗拥有“可以在 2035 年之前开始开发军事上可行的洲际弹道导弹”的导弹技术，但没有说伊朗已经这样做了。理解这个时间表至关重要，因为要用终极武器打击美国，伊朗必须部署一个洲际弹道导弹顶部的核弹头。特朗普坚称，这种威胁并不遥远。 3 月 16 日，他在椭圆形办公室对记者表示，伊朗“将接管中东。他们将用核武器消灭以色列”。慈善解读可能是特朗普认为伊朗想要使用核武器。但愿望，甚至意图，并不等于能力。确实，伊朗拥有铀，如果进一步浓缩，最终可以用来制造核武器。但6月下旬，美国轰炸机袭击了伊朗的核相关设施，加巴德在向国会提交的书面声明中表示，伊朗“自那时起就没有努力重建其浓缩能力”。 “被炸的地下设施的入口已被掩埋并用水泥封闭。”这并不是一个处于使用核武器边缘的国家的景象。特朗普不仅错误地披露了有关伊朗军事潜力的情报。他对该政权对美国和以色列轰炸的反应表示惊讶，特别是伊朗关闭霍尔木兹海峡以及对其波斯湾邻国发动的重型无人机和导弹袭击。但总统的顾问告诉他这很可能会发生。他们知道，限制航运动脉将使伊朗扼杀世界经济。这是一种显而易见的机动，五角大楼已将其纳入其战争规划中。当特朗普的军事顾问告知他这种可能性时，他似乎对此不屑一顾。据《华尔街日报》报道，他说，伊朗在试图关闭该海峡之前可能会投降，无论如何，他认为军方可以应对。 在威胁如果不允许船只自由航行就轰炸伊朗后，特朗普现在表示其他国家应该承担重新开放该航道的责任。特朗普在周三向全国发表的黄金时段讲话中表示：“美国几乎不通过霍尔木兹海峡进口任何石油，未来也不会进口任何石油。” “我们不需要它。”此番言论后油价上涨。特朗普还表示，没有人告诉他伊朗可能袭击沙特阿拉伯、阿联酋、卡塔尔和其他海湾国家，这些国家是美国的亲密盟友并拥有重要的军事基地。特朗普在 3 月 16 日的白宫活动中表示：“他们不应该攻击中东所有其他国家。没有人预料到这一点。我们感到震惊。”他们怎么可能这么做？ 2025年，美国情报界公开报道称，“伊朗的大型常规部队有能力对攻击者造成重大损害，实施区域打击，并扰乱霍尔木兹海峡的航运，特别是能源供应。”国防部长皮特·赫格斯（Pete Hegseth）或许是政府中对这场战争最大的支持者，他不得不承认，伊朗的地区报复并不令人意外。他在 3 月 10 日的新闻发布会上表示：“我不能说我们一定预料到他们会做出这样的反应，但我们知道这是有可能的。”据《Politico》报道，战前，两个阿拉伯国家的官员告诉特朗普及其高级助手，他们担心伊朗可能对他们发动反击，以阻止石油流动、推高价格并引发全球经济危机。 2月初，当美国军舰进入就位时，我会见了卡塔尔的几位高级政府官员。伊朗报复的可能性是首要考虑的问题。一位官员指出，显而易见的是，战争可能使卡塔尔无法生产和运输液化天然气，而液化天然气是其经济的基础。这正是发生的事情。美国在欧洲最亲密的情报共享伙伴之一在进行自己的兵棋推演后确定，美国的一次重大袭击将迫使伊朗袭击海湾国家并试图关闭海峡，该政府的一位官员最近告诉我，该官员要求匿名，以讨论一项敏感评估。这位官员表示，美国人知道这些结论，他对特朗普声称感到惊讶感到困惑。当加巴德上个月出现在参议院情报委员会成员面前时，他们也感到困惑和愤怒。缅因州无党派参议员安格斯·金表示，“就伊朗的这一行动而言，情报界多年来的报道与总统所说的似乎存在差异”。 “我的问题是，你告诉他了吗？”加巴德避免直接回答。但她表示，她监管的机构“之前并持续向特朗普提供了与伊朗这次行动有关的情报”。当时也在场的中央情报局局长约翰·拉特克利夫表示，他参加了“数十次与总统的简报会”，包括在战前几周。他强调“伊朗有具体计划来打击美国在该地区能源基地的利益。”加巴德支持他，指出“情报界长期以来一直在评估伊朗可能会掌握霍尔木兹海峡作为筹码”，这是情报界的简写。参议员们也很想了解为什么加巴德的一位高级副手因总统决定发动战争而辞职。 “伊朗对我们的国家没有构成迫在眉睫的威胁，”特朗普提名负责国家反恐中心的乔·肯特在辞职信中写道，这是一位能够接触到美国政府一些最高度机密情报的官员的揭露性声明。拉特克利夫告诉委员会，他不同意肯特的观点，伊朗仍然渴望制造核武器。但这与实际建造并准备使用它并不相同，正如特朗普声称的伊朗正在做的那样。 阅读：战争中的陆军改组 来自佐治亚州的民主党参议员乔恩·奥索夫（Jon Ossoff）大声宣读了战争开始后第二天白宫声明的一部分：特朗普已下令“采取军事行动，消除伊朗政权构成的迫在眉睫的核威胁”。他问加巴德：情报界是否评估过威胁迫在眉睫？这位情报总监在担任国会议员时曾采取强烈的反战立场，但他的路线却很尴尬。她告诉奥索夫，总统是“唯一能够确定什么是迫在眉睫的威胁、什么不是迫在眉睫的威胁的人”，而这样做不是情报部门的工作。事实上，做出这一决定正是情报界的工作。但抛开加巴德回避的描述不谈，她表示，“伊朗仍然有重建和继续增强其核浓缩能力的意图。”她没有提到的是：意图和迫在眉睫的威胁之间存在着天壤之别。许多总统都忽视了情报顾问的警告和预测，或者根本没有时间听取他们的意见。 1994 年，当一架被盗的塞斯纳飞机在白宫南草坪坠毁时，一些人开玩笑说，驾驶这架飞机的是比尔·克林顿 (Bill Clinton) 的中央情报局简报员，拼命地想与总统会面。另一方面，乔治·W·布什则痴迷于反恐行动的细节，跟踪中央情报局正在追捕和杀害的各种基地组织成员。特朗普与情报界的关系比他的任何前任都更加令人担忧。作为候选人，他严厉斥责这些机构对伊拉克大规模杀伤性武器的拙劣呼吁。作为总统，他一直谴责“深层政府”，他声称这些“深层政府”十多年来一直在暗中陷害他。特朗普长期以来一直表示他相信自己的直觉。他最近告诉一位采访者，“当我感觉到它时，我会在我的骨头里感觉到它，他就会知道伊朗战争已经结束。”美国情报界既没有设计也没有能力限制一位被冲动、情绪和自己的感情所感动的总统。它只能为他提供信息。当总统无视他所告知的内容或歪曲其内容时，失败只能由他一人承担。

Original Summary

  1. In 2005, a bipartisan commission of lawmakers and security experts concluded that “the Intelligence Community was dead wrong in almost all of its pre-war judgments about Iraq’s weapons of mass destruction.” America’s spies had told President George W. Bush that Saddam Hussein had reconstituted a nuclear-weapons program and that Iraq possessed biological weapons and mobile production facilities, as well as stockpiles of chemical weapons. These supposed facts became the basis for a U.S. invasion and an eight-year occupation. “Not one bit of it could be confirmed when the war was over,” the commission found. “This was a major intelligence failure.”If a similar panel of experts scrutinized the run-up to the current war in Iran, their assessment might go something like this:The intelligence community was accurate and consistent in its prewar judgments about Iran’s capabilities and intentions to attack the United States and its allies. Contrary to what President Trump has said to justify his decision, the intelligence showed that the Iranian regime was not preparing to use a nuclear weapon; it did not have ballistic missiles capable of reaching the United States; and in response to a U.S. military attack, Iran was likely to strike at neighboring countries in the Persian Gulf and try to close the Strait of Hormuz, precipitating a global economic crisis. All of this was known before the war and presented to President Trump. This was an intelligence success.Trump’s “excursion,” as he calls the biggest U.S. military operation of his second term, has unleashed a parade of horribles. Iran now controls the strait, where it plans to charge vessels a toll and can govern global flows of oil, natural gas, fertilizer, and chemicals that are crucial for manufacturing. A regime that Trump claims to have replaced still remains in the hands of hard-liners, whose repression of the Iranian people will be strengthened for having survived a decapitation strike by the world’s only superpower. And neighboring countries in the Gulf, whose livelihoods depend on exporting energy and creating safe places for people to visit, live, and work, will amass new weapons and reconsider their strategic partnerships with the United States.Two decades ago, a president embraced information that turned out to be wrong, and disaster followed. Today, a president disregards assessments that proved to be right, and the predictable comes to pass. There’s a failure of intelligence there too—just not the kind we’re used to seeing.“Your successes are unheralded—your failures are trumpeted,” President John F. Kennedy remarked in a speech to CIA staff at their headquarters, in Langley, Virginia, in 1961. Ever since, intelligence officers have ruefully invoked that truism whenever they’re blamed for a major screwup. The familiar storyline of an intelligence failure features analysts who neglect to “connect the dots,” case officers who get seduced by sources who exaggerate or lie, and politicians who contort ambiguous information to align with their preferred outcome. That’s what happened in the months before the Iraq War.The lead-up to Operation Epic Fury turns this narrative on its head. The spies called it right, but the president went another direction. The failures of the intelligence community on Iraq’s WMDs produced systemic changes meant to keep botched calls like that one from recurring. In many respects, those reforms have worked. But they couldn’t account for a decision maker who had been seduced by previous military successes into thinking that the U.S. armed forces, under his inspired and perhaps divinely endowed command, could never stumble.Some of Trump’s allies have criticized him for not making a public case for war, as the Bush administration did. But if he had accurately presented the intelligence, the facts would have argued against attacking Iran—or at least for not striking before the diplomatic options had been exhausted. Perhaps that’s why the president ignored, and later misrepresented, what his advisers told him.Read: Trump’s fateful choice“The regime already had missiles capable of hitting Europe and our bases, both local and overseas, and would soon have had missiles capable of reaching our beautiful America,” Trump said before a Medal of Honor ceremony at the White House on March 2. But the Defense Intelligence Agency had concluded that building a missile that could hit the United States would take Iran until 2035, and only then if it was determined to do so, which analysts concluded it was not. When Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard—hardly the model of an apolitical presidential adviser—testified before Congress a few weeks later, she reported that Iran had missile technology that “it could use to begin to develop a militarily viable ICBM before 2035,” but did not say that it had done so. That timeline is crucial to understand, because to hit the United States with the ultimate weapon, Iran would have to place a nuclear warhead on top of an intercontinental ballistic missile.That threat was not years away, Trump insisted. Iran was “going to take over the Middle East. They were going to knock out Israel with their nuclear weapon,” he told reporters in the Oval Office on March 16. A charitable reading might be that Trump believes Iran wants to use a nuclear weapon. But desire, or even intention, does not equal capability.It’s true that Iran possesses uranium that could eventually be used to build a nuclear weapon, were it to be further enriched. But in late June, U.S. bombers struck nuclear-related facilities in Iran, which had made “no efforts since then to try to rebuild their enrichment capability,” Gabbard said in her written statement to Congress. “The entrances to the underground facilities that were bombed have been buried and shuttered with cement.” That’s not a picture of a country on the brink of using a nuclear weapon.Trump not only has misstated intelligence about Iran’s military potential. He has expressed surprise at the regime’s response to American and Israeli bombing, particularly Iran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the heavy drone and missile attacks it has launched on its neighbors in the Persian Gulf. But the president’s advisers had told him this was likely to happen. They knew that restricting a shipping artery would give Iran a chokehold on the world’s economy. It’s such a no-brainer maneuver that the Pentagon has built it into its war planning. When Trump’s military advisers apprised him of this possibility, he appeared to have shrugged them off. Iran would probably capitulate before trying to close the strait, he said, and in any event, he thought the military could handle it, The Wall Street Journal reported.After threatening to bomb Iran if ships weren’t allowed to travel freely, Trump now says other nations should bear the burden of reopening the waterway. “The United States imports almost no oil through the Hormuz Strait and won’t be taking any in the future,” Trump said in a primetime address to the nation on Wednesday. “We don’t need it.” Oil prices rose following his remarks.Trump has also said that no one told him that Iran was likely to attack Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, and other Gulf nations that are close allies of the United States and host vital military bases. “They weren’t supposed to go after all these other countries in the Middle East,” Trump said during a White House event on March 16. “Nobody expected that. We were shocked.”How could they be? In 2025, the U.S. intelligence community publicly reported that “Iran’s large conventional forces are capable of inflicting substantial damage to an attacker, executing regional strikes, and disrupting shipping, particularly energy supplies, through the Strait of Hormuz.” No less than Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth, perhaps the war’s biggest cheerleader in the administration, had to admit that Iran’s regional retaliation was not exactly a surprise. “I can’t say that we anticipated necessarily that’s exactly how they would react, but we knew it was a possibility,” he said at a press conference on March 10.Before the war, officials from two Arab countries told Trump and his top aides that they worried Iran could launch counterattacks on them, in order to halt the flow of oil, drive up prices, and trigger a global economic crisis, Politico reported. In early February, as U.S. warships were moving into position, I met with several of Qatar’s senior government officials. The likelihood of an Iranian reprisal was top of mind. One official pointed out the obvious, that a war could make it impossible for Qatar to produce and ship liquefied natural gas, the foundation of its economy. That’s exactly what happened.After conducting its own war-gaming, one of the United States’ closest intelligence-sharing partners in Europe determined that a major American attack would compel Iran to hit countries in the Gulf and try to close the strait, an official in that government recently told me on the condition of anonymity to discuss a sensitive assessment. The Americans were aware of those conclusions, according to the official, who was baffled that Trump claimed to be surprised.Members of the Senate Intelligence Committee were also nonplussed, and angry, when Gabbard appeared before them last month. “There seems to be a discrepancy between what the intelligence community has reported over the years and what the president has said in terms of this action” in Iran, Senator Angus King, an independent from Maine, said. “And my question is, did you tell him?”Gabbard avoided answering directly. But she said that the agencies she oversees had provided Trump “with the intelligence related to this operation in Iran, before and on an ongoing basis.” CIA Director John Ratcliffe, who was also present, said that he had participated in “dozens and dozens of briefings with the president,” including in the weeks before the war. He emphasized that “Iran had specific plans to hit U.S. interests in energy sites across the region.” Gabbard backed him up, noting that “this has long been an assessment of the IC that Iran would likely hold the Strait of Hormuz as leverage,” using a shorthand to refer to the intelligence community.Senators were also keen to understand why one of Gabbard’s top deputies had quit his job over the president’s decision to go to war. “Iran posed no imminent threat to our nation,” Joe Kent, whom Trump had nominated to run the National Counterterrorism Center, wrote in his resignation letter, a revealing statement from an official who had access to some of the most highly classified intelligence in the U.S. government. Ratcliffe told the committee that he disagreed with Kent and that Iran maintained an aspiration to build a nuclear weapon. But that is not the same thing as actually building one and preparing to use it, as Trump has claimed Iran was doing.Read: An Army shake-up in the middle of a war Senator Jon Ossoff, a Democrat from Georgia, read aloud a portion of a White House statement from the day after the war began: Trump had ordered “a military campaign to eliminate the imminent nuclear threat posed by the Iranian regime.” He asked Gabbard: Had the intelligence community assessed that the threat was imminent?The intelligence director, who had taken passionately anti-war stances as a member of Congress, walked an awkward line. She told Ossoff that the president is “the only person who can determine what is and is not an imminent threat,” and that doing so was not the intelligence community’s job. In fact, it is precisely the job of the intelligence community to make that determination. But putting Gabbard’s evasive characterization aside, she said that “Iran maintained the intention to rebuild and to continue to grow their nuclear enrichment capability.” What she didn’t mention: There is a world of difference between intention and imminent threat.Plenty of presidents have dismissed the warnings and prognostications of their intelligence advisers, or simply not made time to hear them. When a stolen Cessna crashed on the South Lawn of the White House in 1994, some joked that it was flown by Bill Clinton’s CIA briefer, trying desperately to get a meeting with the president. At the other end of the spectrum, George W. Bush became obsessed with the minutiae of counterterrorism operations, keeping track of the various al-Qaeda members whom the CIA was hunting and killing.Trump’s relationship with the intelligence community is more fraught than any of his predecessors’. As a candidate, he excoriated the agencies for their botched call on Iraq’s WMDs. As president, he has railed against a “deep state” that he claims has been out to get him for more than a decade. Trump has long said that he trusts his gut. He’ll know the war in Iran is over, he recently told an interviewer, “when I feel it, feel it in my bones.”The U.S. intelligence community is neither designed nor equipped to restrain a president who is moved by impulse, emotion, and his own feelings. It can only provide him with information. When the president disregards what he’s told, or distorts it, that failure is his alone.

这根本不是真的。情报界报告称不存在大规模杀伤性武器，但随后白宫介入分析，将政治因素纳入其中，并更改了报告。

> America’s spies had told President George W. Bush that Saddam Hussein had reconstituted a nuclear-weapons program and that Iraq possessed biological weapons and mobile production facilities, as well as stockpiles of chemical weapons.

That's not true at all. The intelligence community reported that there were no weapons of mass destruction but then the White House got involved in the analysis and brought politics into it and changed the reports.

在 HN 中查看
lordnacho 2026/04/06 20:48
从表面上看，这篇文章是正确的，情报部门并没有犯错，尽管有很好的评估，但政府还是做出了错误的决定。

但实际上，这是价值观的失败。

想要做出对美国及其朋友有利的决定是一种价值观。人们曾经说过，把你应该代表的人置于你自己之上是很重要的事情。过去，领导人试图证明他们已经仔细考虑过自己的决定。

一旦你有一个把自己置于一切之上的政府，这整件事就有意义了。

这届政府充满了没有安全感的人，他们想展示自己有多么强大。你可以从他们的谈话方式以及来自白宫的源源不断的表情包中看到这一点。这是非常幼稚的，就像特朗普被描绘成六块腹肌，殴打他的敌人一样。

强人政权倾向于试图偷盲，用扑克牌的概念来说：通过采取超级激进的行动来胁迫对手让步。就像从火车轨道上捡硬币一样，大多数时候你会赢，而对手会退缩，即使在纸面上对手往往拥有更好的牌，因为理性的对手会喜欢限制风险。最后一点非常重要，因为这意味着欺凌者知道尽管拒绝建议，他仍然可以获胜。

所以你可以到处打人，直到它停止工作，而伊朗很有可能就是它停止工作的地方。如果不是伊朗，那就是下一个，因为他们无法阻止。

回到价值观，太多美国人不愿意为自己国家的行为承担责任。如果你看看是什么导致了人们对伊朗当前局势的不满，那就是天然气价格之类的事情。换句话说，仍然是自利。

Superficially, the article is right, intelligence services didn't get this wrong, and the administration made a bad decision despite having a good appraisal to hand.

But really, it's a values failure.

Wanting to make decisions that are good for America, and good for its friends, is a value. Putting people you are supposed to represent ahead of yourself used to be the kind of thing people would say mattered. It used to be a thing that leaders tried to demonstrate that they had carefully considered their decisions.

Once you have an administration that puts itself ahead of everything else, this whole thing makes sense.

This administration is full of insecure people who want to show how strong they are. You can see it in how they talk, and the constant stream of memes coming from the WH. It's incredibly juvenile, stuff like having Trump portrayed with a sixpack, beating up his enemies.

Strongman regimes have a tendency to try to steal the blind, to use a poker concept: bully the opposition into giving you a concession, by making super aggressive moves. Like picking pennies off a train track, most of the time you will win and the opponent will back down, EVEN if on paper the opponent tends to have the better cards, because a rational opponent will appreciate putting a lid on risk. This last bit is really important, because it means the bully learns that he can win despite rejecting advice.

So you can go around sucker punching people until it stops working, and there's a decent chance Iran is where it stops working. If it's not Iran, it will be the next thing, because they can't stop.

And to get back to values, too many Americans are unwilling to take responsibility for their country's actions. If you look at what causes discontent with the current Iran situation, it is things like gas prices. In other words, self-interest, still.

在 HN 中查看
ben_w 2026/04/07 07:09
的确。

对我来说，有两个悬而未决的问题是：高空电磁脉冲究竟有多危险？拥有核武器的国家是否真的在战争游戏中模拟了这一点？

我看到它表明，即使是相对温和的 HAEMP 也会对美国大陆的大多数变压器造成物理损坏，因此需要同时更换所有变压器，因为它们都是定制的并且有逐年的交货时间。没有电、没有燃油泵、没有制冷，美国 90% 的人口一年内就会挨饿。

自 1989 年 3 月的地磁风暴暴露出这种弱点以来，美国电网本可以在几十年内得到保护，但考虑到各种证据，例如加利福尼亚州因百年老电线故障而引发的火灾，这些电线早该修复，我怀疑它们是否已经得到保护。

Indeed.

One thing that's an open pair of questions for me is: exactly how dangerous is a high-altitude EMP anyway? And do countries with nuclear weapons actually model this in war games?

I've seen it suggested that even a relatively modest HAEMP would be able to physically damage most transformers in the continental USA, necessitating replacement of all of them at the same time when they're all custom and have month-to-year lead times. No electricity, no fuel pumps, no refrigeration, in the US 90% of the population starves within a year.

The US power grid could have been defended in the decades since the March 1989 geomagnetic storm revealed such weakness, but given various evidence such as the fires in California caused by failure of century-old power lines that were well past due for repair, I doubt they have been.

gregw2 2026/04/06 21:00
我抛出这个观察更多的是为了具有启发性而不是说服性，但我在其他地方没有看到过。

在我之前的人已经观察到特朗普的举动都是由自我驱动的，或者是自私的，或者是为普京、以色列或天然气公司服务，而我在这里添加了一个不同的猜想。

特朗普的举动都倾向于以一种似乎可以否认的方式加剧通货膨胀。关税、联邦斗争、战争等。

这虽然极不受欢迎，但在精英看来是处理美国国债的必要条件。

通货膨胀使富裕阶层能够在不承认/追求紧缩政策的情况下扩大政府支出，这在卡特领导下是政治自杀。

富人将土地和股票投资组合作为庇护所，这些投资组合与现金不同，这些投资组合不断增长，并且也从上述支出中受益，而普通人则支付额外的累退税，即通货膨胀。然后精英们就可以转身指责这个支持特朗普的小人物，他们的手就干净了。

I throw out this observation more to be provactive than persuasive, but I haven't seen it elsewhere..

People before me have observed how Trump's moves all are ego driven, or self serving or serve Putin or Israel or gas companies, and I'm here to add to the mix a different conjecture.

Trump's moves all tend to increase inflation in a plausibly deniable way. Tarrifs, fed-fighting, wars, etc.

And that is a deeply unpopular but elite-viewed necessity for handling America's national debt.

Inflation allows the wealthy class to get away with extending government spending without admitting/pursuing austerity which was political suicide under Carter.

The wealthy shelter in their land and stock portfolios which keep growing unlike cash and also benefit from said spending, while ordinary people pay the extra regressive tax that is inflation. The elite can then turn around and blame the little guy for supporting Trump and their hands are clean.

在 HN 中查看
jillesvangurp 2026/04/06 21:08
很可能这是经过计算且令人满意的结果。半个多世纪以来，关闭该海峡的可能性一直是情报报告的主题。

人们谈论了很多关于特朗普的话题。但他当然不是单独行动。这里的大局是，这并不像看起来那么冲动，并且得到了为他管理日常政策的团队的热情支持。当然，令人担忧的是事情并没有完全按计划进行。这本身是完全可以预见的，并且从第一天起就被广泛预测。但这里的要点是，特朗普是一个傀儡，只关注他是一个错误。跟着钱走。

他的总统职位是花钱买来的。部分是石油资金以及随之而来的对中东的强硬路线。第一次和第二次海湾战争背后的财力同样雄厚。我们不妨开始将其称为第三版。

其中很大一部分资金来自德克萨斯州。所有石油和天然气都在那里。德克萨斯州出口石油和天然气。如果霍尔木兹海峡关闭，这些东西就会变得更有利可图。看起来重建基础设施可能需要几年时间。恕我直言，他们高估了他们在这方面的成功程度，并且由于石油驱动的通货膨胀而产生的所有破坏性影响（例如马加支持者对特朗普感到愤怒）可能会适得其反。但如果你只关注金钱，很容易看出决策会如何进行。

Chances are that this was a calculated outcome that was found desirable. The potential closure of that strait has been the topic of intelligence reports going back more than half a century.

People talk a lot about Trump. But he's of course not acting alone. The big picture here is that this wasn't as impulsive as it may seem and was rather enthusiastically supported by the team that runs day to day policy for him. The worrying thing of course is that things aren't exactly going as planned. Which by itself was entirely predictable and widely predicted from day 1. But the point here is that Trump is a stooge and focusing on just him is a mistake. Follow the money.

His presidency was bought and paid for. Partially with oil money and the accompanying hard line towards the middle east. Those are the same deep pockets behind the Gulf wars I and II. And we might as well start referring to this one as edition III.

A lot of that money comes from Texas. Where all the oil and gas is. Texas exports oil and gas. That stuff becomes more lucrative if the Strait of Hormuz is closed. And it looks like rebuilding infrastructure might take a few years. IMHO, they overestimated how successful they were going to be with this and all the disruptive effects (e.g. Maga supporters getting angry at Trump) because of oil driven inflation at the pump might be backfiring a bit. But it's easy to see how the decision making could have gone if you just follow the money.

在 HN 中查看
cbg0 2026/04/06 20:38
> 所有其他欧洲国家都表示不会

这是错误的，法国犯了一个错误，甚至因为未能预测入侵而解雇了法国军事情报部门的负责人，而德国也明显持怀疑态度。英国、波兰、爱沙尼亚、拉脱维亚、立陶宛等其他国家也表示入侵将会发生。

> all other European countries said it wouldn’t

This is false, France made a misstep and even fired the head of French military intelligence because of the failure to predict the invasion and Germany was also notably skeptical. Other countries like UK, Poland, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania were on board that the invasion was going to happen.

在 HN 中查看
jmyeet 2026/04/06 21:19
人们实际上不需要比《每日秀》汇编走得更远就能看到发生了什么[1]。

至于伊拉克，这篇文章是错误的。这是 1998 年写给时任总统比尔·克林顿 (Bill Clinton) 的一封信，敦促他入侵伊拉克 [2]。精明的人会注意到这是 9/11 之前的 3 年。看看签名者。它们包括：

- 唐纳德·拉姆斯菲尔德：乔治·W·布什领导下的未来（和前任）国防部长，负责监督入侵伊拉克；

——保罗·沃尔福威茨。拉姆斯菲尔德是布什领导下的副手，可以说比拉姆斯菲尔德更加鹰派。他公开承认伊拉克“与石油有关”，而大规模杀伤性武器的借口是“官僚主义”[3]；

- 理查德·阿米蒂奇（Richard L. Armitage）：伊拉克战争期间科林·鲍威尔的国务院副手；

- 彼得·W·罗德曼 (Peter W. Rodman)，拉姆斯菲尔德手下的助理国防部长；

还有其他一些名字现在或曾经是有影响力的保守派记者和新保守主义运动的“思想”领袖，例如威廉·克里斯托尔。

不管你怎么说，在伊拉克（或伊朗）这件事上，情报并没有失败。政治目标压倒一切。

[1]： https://www.youtube.com/shorts/JC56Ltg5zDE

[2]： https://noi.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/01/iraqclintonletter...

[3]： https://www.marxists.org/history/etol/newspape/socialist-vie...

One doesn't really need to go much further than this Daily Show compilation to see what happened [1].

As for Iraq, the article is just wrong. Here's a 1998 letter sent to then-president Bill Clinton urging him to invade Iraq [2]. The astute will notice this was 3 years before 9/11. Look at the signatories. They include:

- Donald Rumsfeld: future (and previous) Defense Secretary under George W. Bush who oversaw the invasion of Iraq;

- Paul Wolfowitz. Rumsfeld's deputy under Bush, arguably even more hawkish than Rumsfeld. He openly admitted Iraq was "about oil" and the WMD excuse was "bureaucratic" [3];

- Richard L. Armitage: Colin Powell's deputy at State during the Iraq war;

- Peter W. Rodman, an assistant Defense secretary under Rumsfeld;

There are other names there who are or were influential conservative journalists and "thought" leaders in the neocon movement eg William Kristol.

Whatever else you might say, intelligence didn't fail on Iraq (or Iran) for that matter. Political goals simply trumped everything else.

[1]: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/JC56Ltg5zDE

[2]: https://noi.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/01/iraqclintonletter...

[3]: https://www.marxists.org/history/etol/newspape/socialist-vie...

api 2026/04/07 05:54
为什么人们不能互换角色并看看这是多么愚蠢？

假设美国有一个非常不受欢迎的政权，该政权已经掌权到无法通过选举推翻的程度。

现在假设中国人开始轰炸我们以“解放”我们。

人们会对此感到很酷，对吧？大家都会为中国人加油吧？

Why can't people reverse the roles and see how silly this is?

Let's say the US had a massively unpopular regime that had taken power to the point that they couldn't be removed by elections.

Now let's say the Chinese start bombing us to "liberate" us.

People would be cool with that, right? Everyone would cheer for the Chinese, right?
